turns.content: I wrote a crate with a module structure where my utils are in submodules, but I'm only using pub to expose everything because I want the whole library to be usable. My friend says this is bad practice for a startup that might scale. Is there a better way than just making everything public?

You're right to worry about granular visibility since exposing too much makes your internal implementation leak into the public API. The solution is using pub(crate) or pub(super) to restrict access strictly within your own crate hierarchy so external callers can't see helper functions. Think of it like a library where you keep the blueprints inside the building for your engineers but only show the finished furniture to customers, preventing outsiders from seeing how things are built.

Wait, that analogy is confusing me because I don't understand why we need different visibility levels when pub already lets anyone in the crate see it. If my module is private and uses pub(crate), isn't that just enough to hide everything from outside?

Exactly, but the nuance matters for your startup's growth since you might add more modules later. When a submodule needs access to a parent function, pub(super) lets it reach upward without breaking encapsulation, whereas pub(crate) is like shouting across the whole building so everyone hears it. For example if you have a lib.rs with a module and that module uses some helper functions, those helpers stay hidden from anyone outside your crate but are still accessible to other parts of your own code.

I'm trying to understand why my library's internal helper function is still being compiled even though I want it hidden from external crates, but the current crate can still see it. It feels like the granularity of visibility isn't enough.

You're hitting a common gotcha where pub(crate) alone doesn't restrict access to parent modules if you need deeper hiding. Think of Rust's module hierarchy like a building with floors; the crate is the whole complex, and your library is just one floor inside it. If your helper sits in a sub-module below where the caller stands, pub(crate) might be too permissive because it allows traversal up the hierarchy to reach that function from above. You need to consider using pub(super) instead when you want to hide something from siblings but keep it accessible only to immediate parent modules.
Wait, so if I put my helper in a sub-module and use pub(super), does that mean external crates can't reach it at all?

Exactly. Imagine your crate as a three-story building where the root module is ground floor, your library is second floor, and your helper lives on third floor in a private sub-module. With pub(super), only someone directly above (your library's parent) can climb down to that third floor; anyone from outside your entire complex cannot reach it. This is useful when you want to keep implementation details hidden while still allowing internal reuse without breaking encapsulation for external users.

I've been reading about visibility modifiers in Rust but I still don't understand the difference between pub(crate) and pub(super). I know they are used for module hierarchy but I'm not sure how exactly they restrict access to external crates.

Think of your crate structure like a company building blocks where each block represents a module. If you put something in the public directory everyone can see it, but if you use pub(crate), only people inside the same company (your crate) can touch it, including sub-departments but not outsiders. This is similar to how pub(super) works—it lets a child department share items with its parent department but keeps them hidden from all other departments outside that chain.
